content.text: Progressive disclosure hides complexity behind simple defaults and reveals it only when users demonstrate they need it. For LLM applications, this means the chat input is always visible, model parameters live behind an expandable panel, and developer diagnostics are role-gated or feature-flagged. Applied correctly, it reduces first-session friction without capping what power users can do.

attrs.alt: Three-layer progressive disclosure: always-visible chat UI, expandable advanced settings, developer-mode diagnostics

content.text: Why It Matters for LLM Applications

LLM interfaces are unusually wide in their parameter space: temperature, top_p, max_tokens, system prompts, stop sequences, tool configurations, memory settings. Exposing all of these upfront causes two failure modes:
for new users — they cannot tell what is required versus optional, and default values are not obviously correct.
if everything is hidden by default — experienced users spend time hunting for controls.
The solution is three tiers: default, expandable, and gated. Each tier has progressively higher complexity and progressively smaller user populations.
Input, send/stop, new chat, model selector (2-3 models)
Temperature, system prompt, max tokens, language
Token counts, raw JSON, latency, stop sequences
The boundary between tiers is not technical — it is a judgment call about user goals. A good heuristic: if fewer than 20% of users ever change a setting, it belongs in tier 2 or 3.
The advanced settings panel should be collapsible with a clear visual affordance. Persist the open/closed state to localStorage so users who open it once do not have to reopen it every session.
Developer features should only appear for users who need them. Gate them behind a role or a feature flag rather than hiding them behind a keyboard shortcut that only experts discover accidentally.
Settings from all three tiers contribute to the request. Centralise them in a single settings object rather than scattering state across multiple components.
Collapsible sections must be keyboard-navigable and announce their state to screen readers. The pattern above uses
— the two essential ARIA attributes for disclosure widgets.
, screen readers announce "Advanced settings, collapsed". Avoid using
for the hidden panel — use CSS height/overflow animation or conditional rendering. With
the panel is removed from the accessibility tree, which prevents focus management problems but also means users cannot Tab into controls until the panel is open.

Design LLM UIs with three tiers: always-visible chat primitives, an expandable panel for model parameters (temperature, system prompt, max tokens), and a feature-flagged developer view for raw request inspection. Persist the panel's open/closed state to localStorage so users who expand it once keep it open. Gate tier-3 features with role checks, not hidden keyboard shortcuts. Always wire collapsible panels with
so screen readers announce state correctly.
